ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Asian sea bass (Lates calcarifer); closest_parent used for the sea bass category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sea bass (Dicentrarchus labrax and Lates calcarifer group)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers sea bass as a species group; used as closest_parent as no Singapore-specific Zi Char preparation code exists.

preparation_method: served with soy sauce and ginger

serving.description: 1 whole fish (~1 kg as served at restaurant)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of whole steamed sea bass at a Malaysian Zi Char restaurant is one whole fish of approximately 1 kg, shared among 3–5 diners.

nutrient_source.title: Fish, sea bass, mixed species, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15076
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw sea bass (mixed species) used as the primary nutrient basis. Values represent raw edible flesh per 100 g. Sodium is slightly elevated in the adapted estimate to account for soy sauce used in Zi Char steaming preparation; the raw fish baseline is retained for per_100g values as the preparation contribution is variable and restaurant-specific. IMR Malaysia MyFCD does not have a specific Lates calcarifer entry; USDA SR Legacy is the best available approved source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein-dominant fish foods — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database fish category entries
gi_source.source_match_notes: Sea bass has no direct GI entry in the University of Sydney GI Database. Pure fish and seafood contain negligible digestible carbohydrate and are universally classified as low-GI foods. GI is estimated at 5 by analogy with other lean fish entries in the Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021). Glycemic load per 1 kg serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero for raw fish flesh. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero for plain fish flesh; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15076.

hypertension.key_factor: Low intrinsic sodium in raw fish; preparation-dependent sodium from soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Plain steamed sea bass is low in intrinsic sodium (68 mg per 100 g raw), making it a suitable protein choice for hypertensive individuals, though patients should request reduced soy sauce in Zi Char preparations to limit added sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sea bass contains no digestible carbohydrate and has a negligible glycemic index, making it an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who need to manage postprandial blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean protein with omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sea bass provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids (0.72 g per 100 g) and low saturated fat (0.46 g per 100 g), supporting cardiovascular health; however, patients should limit soy sauce additions to control sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sea bass (Lates calcarifer), known in Malay as Ikan Siakap and in Chinese as 鲈鱼 (Lu Yu), is a popular whole fish served at Malaysian and Singaporean Zi Char restaurants. In the Zi Char context, the fish is typically steamed whole and finished with hot oil, soy sauce, ginger, and spring onion — a preparation that preserves the delicate, mildly sweet flavour and tender, flaky white flesh of the fish. At Marina Seafood House and similar establishments, a whole fish of approximately 1 kg is priced as a single menu item and shared among a table. Nutritionally, sea bass is a lean, high-protein food providing approximately 97 kcal, 18.4 g protein, and only 2.0 g fat per 100 g of raw edible flesh, with zero carbohydrate content. It is rich in selenium (36.5 mcg per 100 g), potassium (328 mg), and phosphorus (211 mg), and provides useful amounts of vitamin B6 and vitamin B12. The low saturated fat content and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids make it a heart-healthy protein choice. Its zero glycemic index makes it ideal for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The primary clinical caution is the sodium contributed by soy sauce in the steaming preparation, which is relevant for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. Sea bass is halal-certified in most Malaysian and Singaporean contexts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in steamed sea bass?
Is sea bass good for diabetics?
Is sea bass high in protein?
Can hypertension patients eat sea bass?
What is 鲈鱼 in English?
Is Zi Char sea bass healthy?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy for raw sea bass (mixed species), which is the best available approved source. IMR Malaysia MyFCD does not contain a specific Lates calcarifer entry. The USDA entry is a strong proxy but is not Singapore- or Malaysia-specific. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database fish category. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero for plain raw fish flesh; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15076. sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as fish contains no plant fibre. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for raw sea bass. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for sea bass (NDB #15076); vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the same source entry. 3. Sodium value (68 mg per 100 g) reflects raw fish flesh only; actual sodium in a Zi Char steamed preparation will be higher due to soy sauce. 4. The input record listed food_group as 'Mixed'; this has been corrected to 'Fish and seafood' as sea bass is unambiguously a fish product. 5. GI set to 5 as an analogous estimate for lean fish; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g is naturally zero. 6. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the cuisine context is Malaysian/Singaporean Zi Char. 7. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for Lates calcarifer in a Zi Char preparation context.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_group from 'Mixed' to 'Fish and seafood'.
Added full multilingual names for Singaporean/Malaysian cuisine context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Sourced nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15076 (raw sea bass).
Documented naturally zero values for carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, dietary_fibre_g, vitamin_c_mg, trans_fat_g.
Documented -1 sentinel values for vitamin_d_mcg and vitamin_e_mg with source justification.
Set GI=5 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fish category; documented basis.
Verified gi_category='low' is correct for GI=5 (≤55 threshold).
Verified all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: exact, closest_parent.
Added specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions including Type 2 Diabetes.
